original_hypothesis.title: BribeFactoryV3.recoverERC20AndUpdateData Calls Wrong Function, Causing Bribe Insolvency
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: BribeFactoryV3.recoverERC20AndUpdateData is intended to recover tokens AND update the Bribe's tokenRewardsPerEpoch accounting. However, it calls `IBribe.emergencyRecoverERC20()` instead of `IBribe.recoverERC20AndUpdateData()`. This means tokens are withdrawn but tokenRewardsPerEpoch is NOT decremented. Subsequent claimants will attempt to claim rewards backed by insufficient token balance, causing later claimants' transfers to revert.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a clear copy-paste bug where the wrong function is called. When admin recovers tokens expecting accounting to be updated, the bribe contract becomes insolvent. Early claimants drain the remaining balance; later claimants get nothing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Function body calls emergencyRecoverERC20 instead of recoverERC20AndUpdateData on bribe contracts

preconditions: Admin calls BribeFactoryV3.recoverERC20AndUpdateData expecting safe recovery
Bribe contract has active rewards for current/future epochs

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: tokenRewardsPerEpoch not decremented after token withdrawal
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin inadvertently creates insolvency by using the 'safe' recovery function
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Early claimants drain remaining balance; later claimants lose all rewards

economic_questions: How likely is admin to use this function vs direct emergencyRecoverERC20?
What is the typical value at risk in bribe contracts?
Is there any secondary safeguard preventing insolvency?
validation_requirements: Confirm the function call mismatch in bytecode
Simulate recovery followed by multiple user claims

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Equal to recovered amount - creates deficit for claimants
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: N/A - admin function bug, not direct attack
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single admin action triggers persistent insolvency

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Affects all claimants of the affected bribe contract

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All voters with unclaimed bribe rewards in affected contract
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Full recovered amount becomes unfunded liability

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify tokenRewardsPerEpoch remains inflated after recovery
investigation_priorities.detection_difficulty: low - function name makes intent clear

validation_result.impact_summary: BribeFactoryV3.recoverERC20AndUpdateData calls emergencyRecoverERC20 instead of recoverERC20AndUpdateData on bribe contracts, withdrawing tokens without decrementing tokenRewardsPerEpoch, causing bribe insolvency for later claimants.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The BribeFactoryV3 contract has two recovery functions: recoverERC20From (lines 106-113) and recoverERC20AndUpdateData (lines 116-123). Both functions have identical bodies - they both call IBribe.emergencyRecoverERC20(). The recoverERC20AndUpdateData function should call IBribe.recoverERC20AndUpdateData() to update the bribe's tokenRewardsPerEpoch accounting, but instead calls the same emergencyRecoverERC20. The local IBribe interface at the top of BribeFactoryV3.sol (line 11) correctly declares both functions, confirming the intent was to call the accounting-aware version. In Bribes.sol, recoverERC20AndUpdateData (lines ~297-304) decrements tokenRewardsPerEpoch[tokenAddress][_startTimestamp] by tokenAmount, while emergencyRecoverERC20 (lines ~308-312) does not. The Bribes.sol code even has a dev comment on emergencyRecoverERC20 warning: 'Be careful --> if called then getReward() at last epoch will fail because some reward are missing! Think about calling recoverERC20AndUpdateData()'. This confirms the two functions have materially different behavior, and calling the wrong one is a code defect.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct comparison of BribeFactoryV3.sol lines 106-113 (recoverERC20From) and lines 116-123 (recoverERC20AndUpdateData) shows identical function bodies. Both call IBribe(_bribe[i]).emergencyRecoverERC20(_tokens[i], _amounts[i]). The recoverERC20AndUpdateData function should call IBribe(_bribe[i]).recoverERC20AndUpdateData(_tokens[i], _amounts[i]). The IBribe interface declared locally in BribeFactoryV3.sol at lines 7-14 includes both function signatures, confirming the correct function is available to call.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This function is intended to recover tokens AND update the bribe's tokenRewardsPerEpoch accounting. However, it calls emergencyRecoverERC20 (which does NOT update accounting) instead of recoverERC20AndUpdateData (which does). This is a copy-paste bug from the recoverERC20From function above it, which has an identical body.

This is the emergency-only recovery function that intentionally does NOT update accounting. Its body is identical to recoverERC20AndUpdateData, confirming the latter was copy-pasted without changing the internal call.

This is the correct function that should be called by BribeFactoryV3.recoverERC20AndUpdateData. It decrements tokenRewardsPerEpoch to maintain solvency. Since it is never called from the factory, using recoverERC20AndUpdateData via the factory will always leave stale accounting.
The dev comment explicitly warns that calling this function will cause getReward() to fail for later claimants. This is the function that BribeFactoryV3.recoverERC20AndUpdateData incorrectly calls.

calling_sequence: 3. For each bribe, calls IBribe(_bribe[i]).emergencyRecoverERC20(_tokens[i], _amounts[i]) -- BUG: should call recoverERC20AndUpdateData
4. Bribes.emergencyRecoverERC20 transfers tokens to owner WITHOUT updating tokenRewardsPerEpoch
5. tokenRewardsPerEpoch remains inflated; bribe contract token balance is reduced
6. Users calling getReward -> earned() computes rewards based on stale (inflated) tokenRewardsPerEpoch
7. Early claimants receive tokens successfully; later claimants' safeTransfer reverts due to insufficient balance
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: BribeFactoryV3.recoverERC20AndUpdateData at line 122 calls IBribe(_bribe[i]).emergencyRecoverERC20(_tokens[i], _amounts[i]). This resolves to Bribes.emergencyRecoverERC20 (Bribes.sol:308-312) which only does safeTransfer without updating tokenRewardsPerEpoch. The correct call would be to Bribes.recoverERC20AndUpdateData (Bribes.sol:297-304) which decrements tokenRewardsPerEpoch[tokenAddress][_startTimestamp] -= tokenAmount before transferring. The earned() function at Bribes.sol lines ~96-117 uses tokenRewardsPerEpoch to calculate user rewards: reward += (cp0.balanceOf * tokenRewardsPerEpoch[_rewardToken][_currTs]) / _supply. Since tokenRewardsPerEpoch is not decremented, users will attempt to claim more than the contract holds.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for BribeFactoryV3 deployment. The bug is in the contract source code itself and is not configuration-dependent.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack per se, but an admin operational bug. When the protocol admin uses recoverERC20AndUpdateData (the 'safe' recovery function) expecting accounting to be properly updated, the function silently calls the wrong underlying function. The admin believes the recovery was done safely, but the bribe contract is now insolvent. Users who claim early drain remaining balance; later claimants get reverted transactions. The admin would need to manually call recoverERC20AndUpdateData directly on each Bribe contract to fix accounting, but the factory batch function is broken.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH impact. When admin performs what they believe is a safe recovery operation via the factory, bribe accounting becomes inconsistent with actual token balances. This causes: (1) tokenRewardsPerEpoch remains inflated after token withdrawal, (2) earned() returns values exceeding actual contract balance, (3) early claimants drain remaining tokens, (4) later claimants' getReward calls revert permanently for the affected epoch. The severity is compounded because the admin explicitly chose the 'safe' function and has no indication anything went wrong until users complain about failed claims.
recommended_mitigations: Add integration tests that verify tokenRewardsPerEpoch is decremented after calling recoverERC20AndUpdateData via the factory
Consider adding a view function to Bribe contracts that checks solvency: sum of tokenRewardsPerEpoch for future epochs <= token balance
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Bribe contract with token0/token1 rewards. 2. Deposit bribe rewards via notifyRewardAmount for current epoch. 3. Have multiple veNFT holders vote (deposit into bribe). 4. Call BribeFactoryV3.recoverERC20AndUpdateData to recover partial reward amount. 5. Verify tokenRewardsPerEpoch is NOT decremented (bug). 6. Have first user claim - succeeds. 7. Have last user claim - reverts with insufficient balance. 8. Compare with direct call to Bribe.recoverERC20AndUpdateData which correctly decrements accounting.
uncertainties: Admin may use direct Bribe.recoverERC20AndUpdateData calls instead of the factory batch function, avoiding the bug in practice
The onlyAllowed modifier on Bribe functions requires caller to be owner or bribeFactory - the factory IS the bribeFactory, so the call path is valid
additional_testing_needed: Verify that the BribeFactoryV3 address is set as the bribeFactory in deployed Bribe contracts (it is, based on the constructor)
Test the exact insolvency scenario with multiple claimants to quantify the impact
Check if there are any off-chain monitoring or admin procedures that would catch the accounting discrepancy
